cards.definition: a large section of Earth's crust that moves slowly on the mantle — plates carry continents and ocean floor
where two tectonic plates meet — three types: constructive, destructive, conservative
plates move apart — magma rises to fill the gap — new crust formed — e.g. Mid-Atlantic Ridge
plates move together — one plate (denser oceanic) subducts under the other — volcanoes and earthquakes
plates slide past each other — crust neither created nor destroyed — major earthquakes e.g. San Andreas Fault
vibrations through the Earth's crust caused by sudden movement of tectonic plates
the point underground where an earthquake originates — directly below the epicentre
the point on the surface directly above the earthquake's focus — experiences most damage
an opening in the Earth's crust where magma, gases, and ash erupt — associated with destructive and constructive boundaries
a logarithmic scale measuring earthquake magnitude — each step is 10 times greater than the previous

questions.question: What is a tectonic plate?
options: A) A section of Earth's mantle
B) A large section of Earth's crust moving slowly on the mantle
C) An underwater mountain range
D) A type of rock formation
questions.explanation: Tectonic plates are the large sections of Earth's lithosphere (crust + upper mantle) that float and move on the semi-molten asthenosphere.

questions.question: What happens at a constructive plate boundary?
options: A) Plates collide and subduct
B) Plates slide past each other
C) Plates move apart — magma rises to create new crust
D) One plate goes under another
questions.explanation: At constructive boundaries, plates diverge — magma rises through the gap, creating new oceanic crust. E.g. Mid-Atlantic Ridge.

questions.question: What is a destructive plate boundary?
options: A) Plates slide past each other
B) Plates move apart
C) Plates converge — denser oceanic plate subducts — volcanoes and earthquakes result
D) No tectonic activity occurs
questions.explanation: At destructive boundaries, the denser oceanic plate is forced under the less dense continental plate — creating volcanoes, earthquakes, and deep ocean trenches.

questions.question: What is the epicentre?
options: A) The point underground where an earthquake begins
B) The point on the surface directly above the focus
C) The edge of a tectonic plate
D) The centre of a volcano
questions.explanation: The epicentre is on the Earth's surface directly above the focus — it experiences the greatest shaking and damage.

questions.question: What is a conservative plate boundary?
options: A) Plates move apart
B) One plate subducts under another
C) Plates slide past each other — no crust created or destroyed
D) Plates are stationary
questions.explanation: Conservative boundaries: plates slide horizontally past each other. The San Andreas Fault (California) is the most famous example — it causes major earthquakes.

questions.question: What does the Richter scale measure?
options: A) Volcanic eruption intensity
B) Earthquake depth
C) Earthquake magnitude on a logarithmic scale
D) Tectonic plate speed
questions.explanation: The Richter scale measures earthquake magnitude — it is logarithmic, meaning a magnitude 7 earthquake is 10 times more powerful than a magnitude 6.

questions.question: What is the focus of an earthquake?
options: A) The surface point of maximum damage
B) The point underground where the earthquake originates
C) The scale used to measure earthquakes
D) The type of plate boundary
questions.explanation: The focus (hypocentre) is the underground point of origin — where rocks suddenly slip. Seismic waves radiate outward from the focus.

questions.question: What type of boundary is the Mid-Atlantic Ridge?
options: A) Destructive
B) Conservative
C) Constructive
D) Collision
questions.explanation: The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is a constructive boundary — North American and Eurasian plates diverge, creating new oceanic crust.

questions.question: Why do volcanoes form at destructive boundaries?
options: A) Plates move apart — magma fills the gap
B) Subducted oceanic plate melts — magma rises through the crust
C) Plates slide past each other — friction melts rock
D) Tectonic plates fold to create mountains
questions.explanation: The subducted oceanic plate melts as it enters the mantle — the magma is less dense and rises through the overriding plate, forming volcanoes.

questions.question: What is a tectonic hazard?
options: A) Any natural disaster
B) A hazard caused by tectonic plate movement — earthquakes, volcanoes, tsunamis
C) A hazard caused by weather
D) A hazard only in developing countries
questions.explanation: Tectonic hazards include earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and tsunamis — all caused by movement of tectonic plates.

questions.question: Tectonic plates move on the mantle.
questions.explanation: Plates float and move on the semi-molten asthenosphere — driven by convection currents in the mantle.

questions.question: New crust is created at destructive plate boundaries.
questions.explanation: New crust is created at CONSTRUCTIVE boundaries. At destructive boundaries, crust is DESTROYED (subducted).

questions.question: The epicentre is underground where the earthquake originates.
questions.explanation: The FOCUS is underground. The EPICENTRE is on the surface directly above the focus.

questions.question: The Richter scale is logarithmic — each step is 10 times greater.
questions.explanation: A magnitude 8 earthquake is 10 times more powerful than a magnitude 7.

questions.question: Conservative boundaries produce volcanoes.
questions.explanation: Conservative boundaries produce EARTHQUAKES — plates slide past each other. Volcanoes form at constructive and destructive boundaries.

questions.question: Destructive boundaries form where oceanic plates subduct under continental plates.
questions.explanation: The denser oceanic plate is forced beneath the less dense continental plate — melting and creating volcanoes.

questions.question: The San Andreas Fault is a constructive boundary.
questions.explanation: The San Andreas Fault is a CONSERVATIVE boundary — the North American and Pacific plates slide past each other.

questions.question: Earthquakes occur at all three types of plate boundary.
questions.explanation: Earthquakes occur at constructive (rifting), destructive (subduction), and conservative (sliding) boundaries.

questions.question: The focus is on Earth's surface directly above where an earthquake originates.
questions.explanation: The EPICENTRE is on the surface. The FOCUS is underground.

questions.question: Volcanoes form where magma rises to the surface.
questions.explanation: Magma rises through weaknesses in the crust at constructive and destructive boundaries.

questions.question: A ________ plate is a large section of Earth's crust moving on the mantle.
questions.explanation: Plate movement drives earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain building.

questions.question: At a ________ boundary, plates move apart and new crust forms.
questions.explanation: The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is a constructive boundary.

questions.question: At a ________ boundary, the oceanic plate subducts — causing volcanoes and earthquakes.
questions.explanation: The subducted plate melts, producing magma that rises as volcanoes.

questions.question: At a ________ boundary, plates slide past each other — earthquakes, no volcanoes.
questions.explanation: The San Andreas Fault is the most famous conservative boundary.

questions.question: The ________ is on the surface directly above where an earthquake originates.
questions.explanation: The epicentre experiences the most severe shaking.

questions.question: The ________ is underground where an earthquake originates.
questions.explanation: Seismic waves radiate outward from the focus.

questions.question: The ________ scale measures earthquake magnitude — it is logarithmic.
questions.explanation: Each step = 10 times greater energy release.

questions.question: ________ hazards include earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis.
questions.explanation: All caused by movement of tectonic plates.

questions.question: Subducted oceanic plates ________ as they enter the mantle — producing magma.
questions.explanation: This magma rises through the overriding plate to form volcanoes.

questions.question: At constructive boundaries, ________ rises to create new crust.
questions.explanation: Magma fills the gap between diverging plates.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: plates move apart — new crust forms — e.g. Mid-Atlantic Ridge
plates converge — subduction — volcanoes and earthquakes
plates slide past — earthquakes only — e.g. San Andreas
surface point above focus — maximum damage
